what_it_does: Defines a static HTML design-system preview card for a nine-tier elevation and shadow scale, showing each shadow token as a rendered chip with labels, y-offset notes, descriptions, and footer guidance.
observations: The file declares dsCard metadata for the Brand group, named "Elevation / shadows", with a "Nine-tier warm-bronze elevation scale" subtitle and 700x220 viewport.
It imports _card.css and defines the preview layout and shadow recipes in an inline style block.
The :root block defines layered CSS custom properties for hairline, resting, xs, sm, md, lg, xl, pop, modal, and gold glow shadows.
Each elevation sample is rendered as a chip on a gridded plinth with an ordinal, name, y-offset summary, and usage description.
Footer notes document the warm color recipe, shared inset top-light, and usage discipline such as tier stepping and avoiding glow plus modal.
There is no JavaScript and no exported code symbols.
standouts: The elevation tokens are expressed as layered box-shadow recipes with contact, ambient bronze, and inset highlight components.
The visual samples pair rendered depth with usage language for interface states and surfaces.
The gold glow tier changes both shadow and chip styling through a gold gradient and border.
conventions: Uses shared design-system variables for fonts, colors, radii, paper, canvas, borders, and foreground tones.
Uses CSS custom properties for reusable shadow tokens.
Uses a repeated tile structure: plinth, chip, row metadata, and description.
Uses warm bronze shadow colors consistently instead of neutral grey shadows.
Keeps the preview self-contained with inline CSS and static HTML markup.
concerns: The grid is fixed to three equal columns with no local responsive media query.
The stated dsCard viewport is 700x220, while the content appears taller than 220px because it includes nine tiles and footnotes.
notes: Brief someone that this file is a static design-system specimen for warm, layered elevation tokens and their intended UI use cases.
questions: Is the 700x220 viewport metadata intended as a capture size even though the rendered card content likely exceeds that height?
Are the shadow custom properties intended only for this preview, or should they mirror shared production tokens elsewhere?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the actual content: it presents a nine-tier warm-bronze elevation and shadow scale.
intention_signals: It seems intended to make elevation feel material and disciplined by pairing precise layered shadow recipes with concrete UI use cases and constraints.
novelty: The file treats shadows as a warm paper-and-bronze material system, including a shared inset top-light and a reserved gold glow tier.
contribution: This file contributes a warm layered elevation vocabulary, visual specimens for nine shadow tiers, and local guidance for mapping depth levels to UI surfaces and interaction states.
summary_for_embedding: Static HTML/CSS preview card documenting and rendering a nine-tier warm-bronze elevation shadow scale for a design system.
intention_for_embedding: This was for communicating how elevation should look and be applied across interface surfaces through tokenized shadows, examples, and usage notes.
